description: Figure 1 presents the engineering stress–strain curves of annealed 2Al (Fe–30.5Mn–2.1Al–1.2C wt.%) and 8Al (Fe–30.5Mn–8.0Al–1.2C wt.%) steels tested in tension at an initial strain rate of 5×10⁻⁴ s⁻¹. Four conditions are compared: 2Al-450°C/1h (black), 2Al-650°C/72h (red), 8Al-450°C/1h (green), and 8Al-600°C/24h (blue). The short-annealed 2Al steel exhibits a yield strength of ~420 MPa, an ultimate tensile strength (UTS) of ~935 MPa, and the highest ductility (~75% elongation). Long annealing of 2Al at 650°C reduces both strength (~850 MPa UTS) and elongation (~35%), which is linked to intergranular M3C carbide precipitation. The 8Al-450°C/1h sample shows ~540 MPa yield strength with ~65% elongation. In contrast, 8Al-600°C/24h achieves the highest strength (~990 MPa yield, ~1180 MPa UTS) but lower ductility (~37%), reflecting effective precipitation strengthening by nanosized j-carbides. These curves demonstrate that Al content and annealing state critically govern the strength–ductility balance in austenitic high-Mn low-density steels.

This figure plots the strain hardening rate versus true stress for annealed 2Al steels (Fe–30.5Mn–2.1Al–1.2C wt.%) tensile-tested at an initial strain rate of 5×10⁻⁴ s⁻¹. The black curve denotes the short-annealed condition (2Al-450°C/1h) and the red curve denotes the long-annealed condition (2Al-650°C/72h). Both samples undergo an initial hardening stage with a decreasing hardening rate, but the short-annealed alloy then enters a distinct second hardening stage starting near 950 MPa true stress and sustains hardening up to fracture above 1600 MPa. Conversely, the long-annealed sample fractures abruptly at approximately 1150 MPa without entering a second hardening stage. Citation context explains that in the long-annealed steel, deformation twinning does not produce additional hardening; instead, impingement of deformation twin bundles on coarse intergranular M₃C-type carbides triggers intergranular fracture, limiting uniform elongation to about 35%.

Figure 3 presents a multi-scale microstructural characterization of the deformation and fracture behavior of long-annealed Fe-30.5Mn-2.1Al-1.2C (2Al) steel. Subfigure (a) is an ECCI image revealing dislocation cell blocks developed at a true strain of 0.2, with a scale bar of 500 nm. Subfigure (b) is an SEM micrograph showing the distribution of intergranular M3C-type carbides along grain boundaries; the caption states these particles are about 1 μm wide and 3 μm long. Subfigure (c) is an ECCI image capturing the interaction between deformation twin bundles and intergranular M3C-type carbides in a sample strained to fracture. Subfigure (d) is a fractography SEM image revealing a predominantly intergranular fracture mode. The associated text reports that impingement of twin tips on these coarse carbides produces local shear stress concentrations, enhancing intergranular cracking. Consequently, deformation twinning in this long-annealed steel triggers a damage mechanism rather than an additional hardening stage, correlating with the reported limited uniform elongation of approximately 35% and fracture at a macroscopic true stress of 1150 MPa.

Figure 4 plots strain hardening rate versus true stress for annealed 8Al steels (Fe-30.5Mn-8.0Al-1.2C, wt.%) tensile deformed at an initial strain rate of 5×10^-4 s^-1. The graph compares two heat-treatment conditions: the black curve represents the short-annealed sample (8Al-450°C/1h), while the red curve represents the long-annealed sample (8Al-600°C/24h). Both curves exhibit an initial sharp drop from approximately 5 GPa into a single hardening regime. The 450°C/1h condition stabilizes near 2 GPa before gradually declining beyond about 1200 MPa true stress. In contrast, the 600°C/24h condition maintains a comparable plateau near 2 GPa but persists to higher stresses, followed by a modest upturn and subsequent drop. This comparison demonstrates that prolonged annealing raises the flow stress range while sustaining moderate strain hardening capacity, supporting the conclusion that uniform intragranular cuboidal j-carbides formed during long-term annealing strengthen the matrix more effectively than the heterogeneous nanosized precipitates present in the short-annealed condition.

This figure presents deformation microstructures of annealed Fe-30.5Mn-8.0Al-1.2C (8Al) austenitic steels investigated by ECCI and dark-field TEM. Panel (a) is an ECCI image showing Taylor lattice structures in a short-annealed (450 °C/1 h) 8Al steel tensile-deformed to 0.3 true strain. Panel (b) is a dark-field TEM image revealing a heterogeneous distribution of intragranular nanosized j-carbides in the short-annealed 8Al sample, with the inset showing a selected-area electron diffraction pattern. Panel (c) is an ECCI image of a long-annealed (600 °C/24 h) 8Al steel deformed to 0.15 true strain, where arrows indicate planar dislocation arrays spaced between 100 and 300 nm. Panel (d) is a dark-field TEM image displaying a uniform distribution of intragranular cuboidal j-carbides with an average size of 10–20 nm in the long-annealed 8Al sample, with arrows marking sheared particles and narrow matrix channels of about 2–3 nm; the inset diffraction pattern confirms a cube/cube orientation relationship with the austenite matrix. The figure demonstrates that both nanosized and cuboidal j-carbides are crystallographically sheared by dislocations, acting as weak obstacles that enhance planar slip. The long-annealed precipitation state produces a remarkable strength increase (YS: 990 MPa) while maintaining considerable ductility (UE: 37%), supporting the conclusion that shearable j-carbides and the associated planar dislocation substructures underpin the superior strength–ductility combination.
